coredata.dc:title: A multifunctional sensing of two carbon dots based on diaminonaphthalenes for detection of ofloxacin drug
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Rateb, Alaa
Ghubish, Z.
Abdel Hakiem, Ahmed F.
El-Kemary, M.
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained increased attention due to their diverse properties in the development of sensors and visualizing dynamic processes. This contribution demonstrates the synthesis of two carbon quantum dots, 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs from 2,3-Diaminonaphthalene and 1,8-Diaminonaphthalene, respectively by hydrothermal process. The results showed that the two CQDs had distinct optical properties with different sizes and therefore we observed different sensing performance and different dynamics interactions, which is dependent on the geometrical structure of precursors. Both CQDs exhibit bi-exponential decay curves with long and short-lived decay components, corresponding to recombination from the defect and intrinsic states, respectively. The sensing performance of 2,3-CQDs which possess high fluorescence quantum yield (0.2) demonstrates an ability to detect Ofloxacin based on fluorescence-enhanced turn off- on with a low detection limit of 0.46 μM and good linear range of 0–120 μM. Meanwhile the sensing of 1,8-CQDs with a quantum yield of 0.17 was performed for detection of Ofloxacin based on fluorescent turn-on–off due to energy transfer mechanism with wide linear response range of 0–120 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 24 nM. These results indicate that we can adapt our systems to the intended applications by specifically and selectively altering carbon dots.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Diaminonaphthalene
Fluorescence
Sensor
Ofloxacin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots with multiple emission
Fluorescence quantum yield and time-resolved fluorescence spectra
Detection of ofloxacin by 2,3-CQDs fluorescence enhanced sensor
Detection of Ofloxacin by 1,8-CQDs fluorescence quenching sensor
Detection OFL in a sample of human blood serum
Ethics approval and consent to Participate

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge Dr. Moatz Rashad, Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University for his support in TEM and SEM measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A, B) TEM image and (C, D) the particle size distribution histogram of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs, respectively.
(A, B) FESEM, (C, D) EDS spectra of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs, respectively.
EDS monocolor element mapping of (A) 1,8-CQDs and (B) 2,3-CQDs.

(A) FTIR spectra of OFL and 1,8-CQDs in acetone/water solution in absence and presence of OFL drug, (B) FTIR spectra of OFL and 2,3-CQDs in acetone/water solution in absence and presence of OFL drug and (C) Absorption spectra of the two CQDs in acetone/water solution and of OFL at pH = 3.5 and the inset of (C) Effect of pH values on the absorption spectra of 2,3-CQDs (D).

The influence of 120 µM of OFL on the absorption spectra of (A)1,8-CQDs and (B) 2,3-CQDs, on the excitation spectra of (C) 1,8-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs/OFL (D) 2,3-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs/OFL in acetone/water. (E) Zeta potential of 2,3-CQDs, 1,8-CQDs, OFL, 2,3-CQD/OFL and 1,8-CQDs/OFL. All the experimental were performed at pH 3.5.
(A, B) The influence of varying excitation wavelength on emission spectra (C, D) and CIE chromaticity of 1,8-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs, respectively.
Lifetime decay of (A) 2.3-CQDs and (B) 1,8-CQDs in absence and presence of OFL.

(A) Statistically quantify the effect of different fluoroquinolone antibiotics on the fluorescence intensity of 1,8-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs, (B) Effect of different fluoroquinolone antibiotics on the absorbance of 1,8-CQDs. Spectral overlap between absorbance of antibiotics drug and emission spectra of (C)1,8- CQDs and (D) 2,3-CQDs.

(A) Effect of pH and temperature on the PL intensity of 2,3-CQDs/OFL, (B) The effect of OFL on the PL spectra of 2,3-CQDs at pH 3.5, (C) Plot of Log(F/F0) vs concentration of OFL Concentration at λex = 375 nm, pH 3.5, and (E) hydrogen bond and static reaction mechanism of 2,3-CQDs/OFL.

(A) Effect of pH and temperature on the fluorescence intensity of 1,8-CQDs + OFL, (B) The quenching of PL emission of 1,8-CQDs by OFL, (C) plot of Log(F/F0)-1 vs concentration of OFL and (D) energy transfer mechanism for fluorescence quenching of 1,8-CQDs by OFL.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of CQDs.
Photoluminescence parameters of the prepared 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs.
Comparison the performance of the proposed sensor's sensitivity with previous OFL detection techniques.

row.entry: Fluorescent system based on aptamer and SYBR Green I

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of OFL in human blood serum.

head.ce:title: A multifunctional sensing of two carbon dots based on diaminonaphthalenes for detection of ofloxacin drug

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University, 33516 Kafr ElSheikh, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University, 33516 Kafr ElSheikh, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, 33516 Kafr ElSheikh, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, 33516 Kafr ElSheikh, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained increased attention due to their diverse properties in the development of sensors and visualizing dynamic processes. This contribution demonstrates the synthesis of two carbon quantum dots, 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs from 2,3-Diaminonaphthalene and 1,8-Diaminonaphthalene, respectively by hydrothermal process. The results showed that the two CQDs had distinct optical properties with different sizes and therefore we observed different sensing performance and different dynamics interactions, which is dependent on the geometrical structure of precursors. Both CQDs exhibit bi-exponential decay curves with long and short-lived decay components, corresponding to recombination from the defect and intrinsic states, respectively. The sensing performance of 2,3-CQDs which possess high fluorescence quantum yield (0.2) demonstrates an ability to detect Ofloxacin based on fluorescence-enhanced turn off- on with a low detection limit of 0.46 μM and good linear range of 0–120 μM. Meanwhile the sensing of 1,8-CQDs with a quantum yield of 0.17 was performed for detection of Ofloxacin based on fluorescent turn-on–off due to energy transfer mechanism with wide linear response range of 0–120 μM and a satisfactory detection limit of 24 nM. These results indicate that we can adapt our systems to the intended applications by specifically and selectively altering carbon dots.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their distinctive structural dimensions and superior physical and chemical capabilities, carbon-based nanomaterials like fullerenes, carbon nanotubes, graphene, graphene derivatives, nanodiamonds, and carbon-based quantum dots have received considerable attention in recent years [1–5]. Novel zero-dimensional fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials called carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have several advantages over other carbon-based nanomaterials, including small size (10 nm), relatively high fluorescence, quick and simple synthesis, good water solubility, biocompatibility, chemical inertness, photostability and widely tunable optical properties. This broadens the application potential of CQDs by enabling their usage in fields like biomedicine, in vivo and in vitro imaging, sensing, and labelling [6].

Due to the numerous functional groups (–OH, –COOH, –NH2, etc.) that have been added to the surfaces of CQDs through a variety of chemical processes, they have a strong ability to connect with both organic and inorganic molecules. Chemiluminescence and electro-chemical luminescence are produced by the remarkable electronic properties of CQDs (electron-donor or electron-acceptor, depending on the chemical structure) [7]. The strong and controllable luminescence of carbon materials further increases their flexible features [8–10].

The rich and distinctive fluorescence feature of CQDs is a result of the structural variety of CQDs. Many studies believe that the complex fluorescent feature of CQDs is mostly caused by the carbon-core states, surface defect states, and molecular states [11,12]. The primary fluorescence mechanism of the vast majority of CQDs is thought to be the surface defect states brought on by different surface functional groups. Excitation dependent emission, multicolor emissions, and complex radiative relaxation are produced by this combination of numerous functional groups. The fluorophores or chromophores on the surface of CQDs cause fluorescent molecular states. The molecular states exhibit behavior like that of organic dyes, which frequently exhibit high quantum yield, sharp, strong fluorescence, and weak photobleaching resistance.

Because antibacterial agents such as fluoroquinolones (FQs) have been widely used in recent years, they have been discovered in surface water, soil, and animal foods such as fish, shrimp, and milk products. Even at low concentrations, they are resulting in a potential public health risk due to their toxicity and allergic reactions, which can cause drug resistance in microbial strains, liver damage, thrombus, central nervous system injury [13,14]. Owing to its broad spectrum of antibacterial activity and ease of oral absorption, ofloxacin (OFL) is one of the most widely used FQs in human and veterinary medicine [15]. Due to its poor metabolism in the human body, approximately 65%–80% of ingested substances are released into the environment in the form of urine and have been consistently identified in the water environment with concentrations ranging between ng L–1 and g L–1, due to its extreme resistance to biodegradation [16]. As a result, effective, sensitive, and robust methods for detecting OFL residue monitoring have become critical.

Numerous analytical methods have been used for the determination of FQS such as liquid chromatography, high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [17], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [18], electrochemistry [19], and surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy [20]. These techniques show high sensitivity and identification ability for targeting analytes, but most of them usually require expensive equipment, well trained staff, and time consuming. Among the various potential techniques photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy are regarded as one of the most promising techniques, due to their fast, simple, and low-cost. PL spectroscopy can overcome the shortcomings of traditional methods, providing an effective technique and promising applications in biomolecules detection [18,21].

In this context, CQDs is one of the most promising materials possess distinguished features as high photo- and chemical stability, biocompatibility, low toxicity, tunable excitation and emission spectra, water solubility, low cost, and simple synthesis process [22]. However, less attention has been devoted to analyzing the effect of substituent positions of the precursors on the photoluminescence behavior of CQDs. Compared with the reported p-phenylenediamine with only one benzene ring, diamino-naphthalene isomers studied here has a larger conjugated structure, which are important characteristics for producing fluorescent CDs and photosensory actions [23].

Here, we demonstrate the preparation of two fluorescence CQDs, synthesized from two different isomers 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (1,8-DAN) and 2.3-diaminonaphthalene (2,3-DAN) with substitutions at α- and β-positions of naphthalene, respectively in acetone/water solution using hydrothermal method. The substitution of NH2 group in two benzene rings of 1,8-DAN at α-positions and in the second condensed benzene ring of 2,3-DAN at β-positions of naphthalene suggesting that the corresponding as-synthesized CQDs may interact differently for sensitive detection of OFL based on fluorescence turn-on–off modes. This owing to efficient intramolecular charge transfer character from α-position to the naphthalene ring compared to that from β-position [24], which is probably due to higher electron density of highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) at α- than β-position [25]. As a result, the electronic structure of produced CQDs changes the system and hence the HOMO/LUMO levels by changing the band gaps [26]. It provides novel insights into the development of robustness, rapid response, and low cost of fluorescent probes with high photoluminescence efficiency.

2,3-Diaminonaphthalene and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene were purchased from Acros Organics. Ethanol and acetone were obtained from Acros Organics. Norfloxacin (NOR), Ciprofloxacin (CIP), Ofloxacin (OFL) were obtained from EIPICO (Egyptian Pharmaceutical Company). Deionized (DI) water was used throughout this work. All Chemicals used were at least of analytical reagent grade and were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots with multiple emission
ce:section.ce:para: The 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs were prepared by using a solvothermal process as described in Scheme 1 . In a typical process, diaminonaphthalene (0.18 g) was dissolved in 28 mL acetone and 12 mL H2O, respectively. The resulting homogeneous solution was transferred to Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated at 180 °C for 12 h. Then, it was cooled at room temperature. The brown or brown-black liquid was produced. After that, the raw material was then subjected to dialysis (3500 Da molecular weight cutoff, alcohol) [27].

The fluorescence quantum yield (Qf) of both 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs at 298 K was determined from the integrated intensity under the emission band using Eq. (1). Quinine sulfate was used as a standard at an excitation wavelength of 365 nm [28].
(1)
∅ f
=
∅
f
ref
n
2
A
ref
n
ref
2
∫
I f
(
λ f
d
λ f
)
∫
I
f
ref
(
I
f
ref
λ f
d
λ f
where n ref and n are the refractive indices of the solvents (1.33), Aref and A (≤0.15) are absorbances at excitation wavelength, Φf and
( ∅
f
ref
= 0.54) are the fluorescence quantum yields of the CQDs and standard, respectively. The integrals denote the area of the fluorescence bands, each parameter for the sample and the standard solution, respectively. Quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution was used as reference. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by exciting the sample at the absorption maximum of the respective compounds. All solutions used for fluorescence measurements had an optical density between 0.05 and 0.15 at the excitation wavelength.

The crystallographic phases of the samples were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, DX-2700). The morphology and size of samples were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM-2100, JEOL) and scanning electron microscopy (JEOL-IT100LA). The sample's absorbance spectra were measured by UV/VIS/NIR JASCO V-770. The FT-IR spectra were obtained using JASCO 6800 spectrometer. The photoluminescence emission spectra were obtained using a JASCO FP-6800 spectrofluorometer. Emission decays were performed using a time-correlated single photon counting approach. The sample was excited at 475 nm using a 40 ps pulsed (20 MHz) laser and the emission signal was collected at the magic angle. The instrumental response function (IRF) was typically 65 ps. Multiexponential functions convoluted with the IRF signal were fitted to the emission decays using Fluofit program. The quality of the fits was characterized using residual distribution and reduced χ2 value.

As shown in Fig. 1 (A, B), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used to characterize the morphology, particle size, and dispersion of the synthesized 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs. The prepared carbon dots are mostly spherical-like in shape, with a uniform dispersion. According to the histogram of particle size distribution obtained by Image J software, the two CQDs have a narrow size distribution ranging from 0.4 nm to 1.65 nm for 2,3-CQDs and 1.25 nm to 3.25 nm for 1,8-CQDs, which may explain their excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior as discussed below, Fig. 1(C, D).

ESEM images revealed that both 1,8-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs had a uniform and regular distribution semi-spherical shape and cubic respectively, as shown in Fig. 2 (A, B). The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) pattern of the two CQDs exhibited similar elements of C, N, and O with different compositions Fig. 2(C, D). These elements, however, are distributed uniformly in the two CQDs samples, Fig. 3 (A, B).

To understand the chemical composition of the samples, we performed the FTIR spectra of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs, in the absence and presence of OFL at pH 3.5, as shown in Fig. 4 (A, B). In the absence of OFL, these two CQDs display the same stretching vibration absorption peaks, such as C=O at 1705 cm−1, OH at 3435 cm−1, N-H at 3250 cm−1, and C-O-C at 1225 cm−1. The peak appeared at 2920 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of C-H, where the peaks corresponding to the C=C, and C–C are observed at 1450 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1, respectively. These results are close to some reported literature values for CQDs and suggest the presence of alkyl and aryl groups, respectively [29]. In the presence of OFL, 1,8-CQDs exhibited high intensity vibration bands of the C=O at ∼ 1705 cm−1, as shown in Fig. 4(A), suggesting the free C=O and lack of hydrogen bond formation between 1,8-CQDs and OFL. In contrast, for 2,3-CQDs/ OFL the observed decrease in intensity and blue shift of the stretching vibration bands of the C=O, OH and NH2 provide insight for hydrogen bonding formation [30]. However, the blue shift provide valuable insight of charge-transfer interaction in the 2,3-CQDs/OFL complex [31].

Fig. 4(C) displays the UV–visible absorption spectra of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs in acetone/water solution and ofloxacin in buffer solution of pH 3.5, as ofloxacin is completely soluble in aqueous solutions with pH 3.5 [32] and there are no significant pH dependents of the component as shown in the inset of Fig. 4(C) as example. The geometrical structure of the CQD precursors determines their optical properties, which were influenced by both the conjugated core and surface electronic states of the CQDs [33]. The absorption spectrum of 2,3-CQDs consists of two bands. The shorter wavelength strong bands at 236 nm and 330 nm correspond to the π-π* transition of the C=C aromatic π-system and the weak one appeared at 400–450 nm, can be assigned to the n– π* transition of oxygen-containing functional groups of 2,3-CQDs in acetone/water solution. These bands traditionally identified as the S2 ← S0 and S1 ← S0 transitions [25]. For 1,8-CQDs, a relatively broad band appeared at 29 nm and 315 nm corresponding to the π-π* transition of where the n– π* transition band appeared at 450–650 nm. It is clear that the UV/vis absorption spectra of 1,8-CQDs observed at longer wavelengths compared to 2,3-CQDs. This is probably due to increasing the conjugated structure of the naphthalene π-systems of 1,8-CQDs, which promoted red shift of the absorption spectra. This is reasonably due to the α-position of naphthalene exhibits relatively high electron density compared with the corresponding β-position [34]. Therefore, enhancing the basicity of the naphthalene π-systems and strength the conjugated structures in CQDs. This π-electrons of the SP2 carbon usually trigger the optical properties of CQDs. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 4(B), the UV–Vis spectra of ofloxacin show a strong band at 286 nm and a weak band at 330 nm, which correspond to the chromosphere involving the nitrogen atom attached to the carboxyl group at position 1 and the nitrogen of the piperazinyl group attached to the 7-carbon to the carbonyl group, respectively [35].

Fig. 5 displays the UV–vis and excitation spectra of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs on the absence and presence of Ofloxacin at pH 3.5. Upon addition of 120 µM of ofloxacin, the absorption and excitation spectra of the two CQDs displayed different spectral changes in both the intensity and the position of the absorption bands. The magnitude of the observed change on the band position depends on the geometrical structure of the CQDs precursors, where both conjugated core and surface electronic states of CQDs contributed to the optical properties. However, we have found that on addition of OFL, the intensity of the absorption peaks at 236 nm increase and showed blue shift in the wavelength of ∼2 nm for 1,8-CQDs and 37 nm for 2,3-CQDs. The spectral change is probably due to the molecular state of the CQDs with different geometrical structure due to different starting precursors. The observed larger spectral shift of 2,3-CQDs compared with 1,8-CQDs up on addition of OFL, might be triggered by the formation of the ground state complex via hydrogen bond and charge transfer of 2,3-CQDs and OFL. Such hydrogen bonding behavior of the CQDs is related to the chemical nature of the surface groups. For 1,8-CQDs, the relatively slightly spectral shift to the blue perhaps arises from steric hindrance of the adjacent amino groups in the α-substituted positions of 1,8-DAN starting material, which hamper the interaction with OFL. In Fig. 5(E), we show the zeta potential plots of OFL, two CQDs and their mixture. The negative charge of OFL can be attributed to the deprotonated COO groups and F- of the OFL at pH 3.5. 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs show broad zeta potential peak centered at +22 and +16 mV respectively at pH 3.5, which can be attributed to the deprotonated NH2. Whereas OFL/1,8-CQDs observed zero charge value which connected to hydrogen bond and charge transfer of NH2 of 1,8-CQDs and C=O of OFL. In addition, the zeta potential of 2,3-CQD/OFL reveal negative charge value at −6.5 are associated to electrostatic interaction [36] and adsorption of OFL on surface 2,3-CQDs.

Fig. 6 (A-D) shows the fluorescence spectra and CIE chromaticity coordinate of two CQDs. 1,8-CQDs displayed broad range of emission wavelengths covering blue, cyan and green, Fig. 6(A, C). However, 2,3-CQDs exhibited blue light, as seen in Fig. 6 (B, D). These changes may be attributed to increasing the conjugated surface structure of 1,8-CQDs, which promotes radiative recombination. Also Carbon-based nanomaterials are well known to have inherent surface defects that can act as surface energy trap state emission, that lead to a decrease in excitation dependent fluorescence intensity for carbon-based nanomaterials in the longer wavelength region. [37–39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield and time-resolved fluorescence spectra
ce:section.ce:para: The value of fluorescence quantum yield (Qf) is crucial for characterization of CQDs because of their potential application in imaging of biological samples and sensing sensitivity. As shown in Table 1 , 2,3-CQDs displayed Qf of 0.20, which is relatively higher than that of 0.17 exhibited for 1,8-CQDs. These values are close to that observed for CQDs (21.5%) performed with urea and ethane diamine, which displayed QY of 21% [40].

To gain insight about the PL mechanism of CQDs, we demonstrated the time-resolved fluorescence decay of 2,3-CQDs and 1,8-CQDs and their interaction with OFL in aqueous solutions upon excitation at 375 nm, Fig. 7 (A, B). The synthesized CQDs were fitted to bi-exponential decays with long and short-lived decay components, corresponding to recombination from the defect and intrinsic states, respectively. Data from the multi-exponential fits are shown in Table 1, together with values for the fluorescence quantum yield (Φf). The result clearly reveals that lifetime is obviously influenced by the precursors. The decay curve of 2,3-CQDs upon excitation at 375 nm was fitted into two decay components, with time constant 3.2 ns (97.60 %) and 0,05 ns (2.40%). However, on the presence of OFL the contribution of the short decay component increased from 2.4% to 6%, probably due to ground state complex formation via hydrogen bonding. However, 1,8-CQD exhibited bi-exponential decay kinetic upon excitation at 375 nm with emission lifetimes of 2.5 ns (39.93 %) and 1.40 ns (60.07 %). When OFL was added to 1,8-CQD, we observed a change in lifetimes and their contributions due to energy transfer. This conclusion is supported by the observed spectral overlap between absorbance of OFL and emission of 1,8- CQDs as discussed later.

The selectivity of 1,8-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs (blank) was performed for detection of antibiotics including Ofloxacin (OFL), ciprofloxacin (CIP), norfloxacin (NOR), levofloxacin (LEVO), lomefloxacin (LOM), and tetracycline (TC). As shown in Fig. 8 (A-D), only OFL, among all the drugs tested, was able to significantly alter the fluorescence spectra, absorbance spectra and overlap between emission and absorbance spectra where the effect of other drugs was relatively weak. These results provided a great opportunity for determination of OFL in human fluids using 1,8-CQDs and 2,3-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ofloxacin by 2,3-CQDs fluorescence enhanced sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9 (A) shows the influence of pH and temperature on the emission spectra of 2,3-CQDs and OFL. The results showed the highest enhanced sensing was obtained atpH 3.5 and 25 °C, due to the better solubility of OFL. Fig. 9(B) shows the enhancement and shift of the emission spectra of 2,3-CQDs with the addition of OFL. The fluorescence enhancement associated with the hydrogen bond interaction accompanied with charge transfer between 2,3-CQDs and OFL [35,41,42]. The fluorescence intensity of the 2,3-CQDs at 375 nm gradually increased as the concentration of OFL increased. The plot of log (F/F0) vs OFL concentration are linear in the concentration range 0.0–120 µM, Fig. 9(C). Where, F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensities of 2,3-CQDs in the absence and presence of OFL, respectively. The detection limit was estimated to be 4.6 x10-7mol L-1. As shown in Fig. 7(A) and Table 1 there is no change in lifetime values of 2,3-CQDs after addition of OFL. This further supports the charge transfer state of the static quenching mechanism, leading to a more stabilized structure. Fig. 9(D) displays the contributions of both charge transfer process and hydrogen bonding ability. Similar behavior was also observed for recently reported CQDs [43,44].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Ofloxacin by 1,8-CQDs fluorescence quenching sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8(C), there is a spectral overlap between the emission spectra of 1,8 CQDs and the absorption spectrum of OFL, suggesting the electronic energy transfer, in which the excited energy transfer from CQDs* to the acceptor OFL. As can be seen from Fig. 5(E), the Zeta potentials of OFL exhibited negative charges (-6.7 mV) while 1,8-CQDs exhibit positive charges (+19 mV) and therefore, can approach one another through electrostatic interaction, which facilitates Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) resulting in optical amplification. This observation is supported by the change in lifetimes of 1,8-CQDs upon addition of OFL, Table 1.

Fig. 10 (A) shows the influence of pH and temperature on the emission spectra of 1,8-CQDs and OFL. The results showed the optimum sensing ability for fluorescence intensity and quenching of 1,8-CQDs was obtained at pH 3.5 and 25 °C. Fig. 10(B) displays the change of emission spectra 1,8-CQDs as a function of OFL concentration in acetone/water solvent. As the Ofloxacin concentration increases, the fluorescence intensity of 1,8-CQDs decreases. The fluorescence quenching was analyzed by the Stern–Volmer quenching plot using the following Stern-Volmer relation:
(1)
F 0/F = 1 + K SV[Q]
where F and F 0 are the fluorescence intensities of the nano-sensor in the absence and presence of quencher of concentration [Q], and K SV is the Stern–Volmer quenching rate constant. Fig. 10(C) shows linear correlation between [(F 0/F) − 1] and [OFL] plot in the concentration range (0.0–120) × 10−6 mol L−1 with R 2 = 0.996. The slope of the straight line equal to K SV = 0.0096 µmol−1 L and standard deviation SD = 0.0077. The detection limit (LOD = from 3SD/K SV) values was calculated to be equal to 0.024 µM [45]. The crucial transfer distance (d0) is the distance at which the probability of intermolecular energy transfer is exactly equal to the sum of the probabilities for all de-excitation processes of the donor's excited state. The value of d0 can be obtained using d0 = 7.35/(C1/2)1/3 = 35A0 or 3.5 nm, which is within the range (2–8 nm), suggesting that 1,8-CQDs is quenched by OFL via Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) process, Fig. 10(D) [43].

To further validate the sensitivity of the recommended methodology, we compared the results reported here with the reported values obtained from other approaches, as shown in Table 2 . It should be noted that among the reported values, the detection of our observed data displays greater sensitivity. Consequently, we can conclude that the used method is useful for detection of OFL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection OFL in a sample of human blood serum
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to apply this method for the detection of OFL in human blood serum as show fluids was investigated. Serum was separated from blood by Centrifuged at a speed of 4000 rpm for 20 min. For luminescence measurements, human Serum was spiked with OFL and CQDs, then diluted with phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 3.5 to 10 mL. The recovery of OFL in human serum samples was obtained using the conventional addition method [52] and the results are summarized in Table 3 .

In the current study, we used 2,3-Diaminonaphthalene and 1,8-Diaminonaphthalene for the synthesis of two novel fluorescent carbon quantum dots by hydrothermal method. TEM images showed that CQDs are spherical with narrow size distribution ranging from 0.4 nm to 1.65 nm for 2,3-CQDs and 1.25 nm to 3.25 nm for 1,8-CQDs, which may explain their excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior. The photoluminescence mechanism of the two CQDs depends on the nature of precursor. The sensitive and selective detection of Ofloxacin using 2,3-CQDs based on fluorescence-enhanced turn off – on due to ground state complex formation. However, the detection of Ofloxacin using 1,8-CQDs was performed based on fluorescent turn- on–off due to energy transfer mechanism. This conclusion is supported by the magnitude of the observed change in lifetime.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ethics approval and consent to Participate
ce:section.ce:para: Not applicable.
Not applicable.
All data generated or analyzed during this study are included in this published article.
Not applicable.

Alaa Rateb: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Z. Ghubish: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ahmed F. Abdel Hakiem: Visualization, Supervision, Formal analysis. M. El-Kemary: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge Dr. Moatz Rashad, Institute of Nanoscience & Nanotechnology, Kafrelsheikh University for his support in TEM and SEM measurements.
